title: Canada’s jobs market expected to chug along even as labour force shrinks
author: Sigma Capital
metaDescription: Canada's jobs market is expected to remain stable despite a shrinking labor force, with modest unemployment rate declines and gradual improvements in per-worker
excerpt: Canada's jobs market is expected to remain stable despite a shrinking labor force, with modest unemployment rate declines and gradual improvements in per-worker conditions. content.introduction: Canada's jobs market is expected to remain stable despite a shrinking labor force, with modest unemployment rate declines and gradual improvements in per-worker conditions. Economists anticipate a gain of about 25,000 jobs in April, following earlier losses. The labor market remains weak, with challenges for job seekers and limited growth outside public sector jobs.
body: Canadian labour market data for April next Friday will be in focus, and we expect employment to remain broadly consistent with a gradual improvement in per-worker conditions after controlling for an unprecedented pullback in labour force growth. That would still leave employment down 70,000 in the first four months of 2026, but would also likely still be enough to push the unemployment rate down to 6.6% from 6.7%—further below the recent 7.1% peak in August and September 2025. The labour market is not yet strong: Unemployment rate declines have still been modest to date, and we don’t expect a spike in wages in March will be repeated in April. A meaningful decline in continuing claims between the March and April reference weeks supports our expectation that the unemployment rate will hold steady at 4.3% in March. Canada’s labour market has continued to look softer on the surface than conditions experienced by individual workers, according to a new analysis from RBC Economics that framed a shrinking labour force as the key to recent unemployment trends. They expect April’s Labour Force Survey to show a gain of about 25,000 jobs, following losses in January and February that only partially reversed in March. “We expect employment to remain broadly consistent with a gradual improvement in per‑worker conditions after controlling for an unprecedented pullback in labour force growth,” Janzen and Fan said. “Employment in sectors heavily exposed to U.S. trade (with 35% or more jobs due to demand from the U.S.) have declined 3% since February 2024, while other sectors grew by 1%,” they said. South of the border, the pair expect US nonfarm payrolls to have added “just 26,000 jobs in March—a number that, given current conditions, represents breakeven growth and has become routine,” with shrinking labour supply meaning “the U.S. does not need substantial job additions to maintain a steady unemployment rate.”.

# Canada’s job market ‘seems to be frozen’: economists say after ‘weak’ March numbers. Canada’s labour market is still fundamentally weak, despite reporting its first employment gain of the year, economists say. He says that with labour supply slowing and labour force growth stalled due to immigration changes, the balance of conditions in the Canadian economy has remained much the same for over a year. While the job gain numbers are a huge relief from the 84,000 job loss in February, this is still not a job seekers market, says Brendon Bernard, senior economist at Indeed. “There’s all the challenges that have built up for Canadian workers looking for new jobs, looking to get into the labour market, youth unemployment,” says Bernard. He says the public sector appears to be holding the hiring market together right now and the only sectors that showed strength over the last year are public administration, healthcare, social assistance, education, and professional and technical services.
